sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Thirteen — factoring polynomials. This is the skill that turns ugly expressions into clean ones, and unlocks solving quadratic equations next module. Four techniques today. We'll go slow, and by the end you'll know which one to reach for every time.

Think about factoring the number twelve. You write it as two squared times three. That's prime factorization — breaking a number into multiplied pieces. Factoring polynomials does the exact same thing for expressions. Why bother? Because factored form REVEALS the solutions and the structure. A polynomial in factored form is one step away from being solved.

Four techniques today. One — greatest common factor, the always-check-first move. Two — trinomials of the form x squared plus b x plus c. Three — difference of squares, a pattern you should be able to spot instantly. Four — grouping, for when you have four terms. Then a strategy slide to help you pick the right tool.

Definition. Factoring means writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials. It is the reverse of distributing. When you distribute, you multiply out. When you factor, you put it back together. Same expression, two different forms — and the factored form is the useful one for solving.

Technique one — greatest common factor. Factor three x squared plus six x. Every term has a three and an x in common. Pull that out front. You get three x times the quantity x plus two. Check by distributing back. Three x times x is three x squared. Three x times two is six x. Matches the original. Done.

Another GCF example. Factor twelve x cubed minus eighteen x squared. The numbers twelve and eighteen share a six. Both terms have at least x squared. So the GCF is six x squared. Pull it out — six x squared times the quantity two x minus three. Always pull out everything you can — the most thorough GCF possible.

Technique two — trinomials. Factor x squared plus five x plus six. You need two numbers that multiply to six AND add to five. Run through the pairs that multiply to six. Two and three work — they also add to five. So the factoring is x plus two, times x plus three. Verify by FOIL — and you get back the original. Beautiful.

Trinomials with negatives. Factor x squared plus two x minus eight. Now you need a pair that multiplies to negative eight and adds to positive two. Try four and negative two — they multiply to negative eight, and they add to two. So the factoring is x plus four, times x minus two. Signs matter — be deliberate with them.
Your turn. Factor x squared plus seven x plus twelve. The hint — find the pair of numbers that multiplies to twelve and adds to seven. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Welcome back. The pairs that multiply to twelve are one and twelve, two and six, three and four. Which pair adds to seven? Three and four — three times four is twelve, three plus four is seven. So the factoring is x plus three, times x plus four. FOIL it back to verify if you want. You got it.

Technique three — difference of squares. This is a pattern. Whenever you see a squared minus b squared, it factors as a minus b times a plus b. Always. Example — x squared minus nine factors as x minus three, times x plus three. Another — four x squared minus twenty-five factors as two x minus five, times two x plus five. Train your eyes to spot this pattern instantly.

Technique four — grouping. Use this for four-term polynomials. Factor x cubed plus two x squared plus three x plus six. Group the first two and the last two. From the first group, pull out x squared — leaving x plus two. From the second group, pull out three — also leaving x plus two. Now both pieces share x plus two — factor that out. Final answer — x plus two, times x squared plus three.

Strategy. How do you pick? Step one — ALWAYS check for a GCF first. Always. Step two — count the terms. Two terms means look for difference of squares. Three terms means trinomial. Four terms means try grouping. Step three — verify by distributing your factors back out. If it matches the original, you're done.

Real-world payoff. A rectangle has area x squared plus five x plus six. What are its dimensions? Factor the area. You get x plus two, times x plus three. So the rectangle is x plus two units wide, by x plus three units tall. Factoring just turned an area expression into actual physical dimensions. That's why this skill matters.

The most common trap. Students see x squared plus nine and try to factor it as x plus three, times x plus three. WRONG. That would give you x squared plus six x plus nine, not x squared plus nine. The truth — a SUM of squares like x squared plus nine cannot be factored over the real numbers. Difference of squares yes, sum of squares no. Pattern matters.

Recap. Factoring is multiplication reversed. Always pull the GCF out first. For trinomials, find the pair that multiplies to the constant and adds to the middle coefficient. Memorize the difference of squares pattern. Grouping is your tool for four terms. And always verify by distributing back. Your assignment — twelve factoring problems mixing all four methods.

How to actually master this module. One — read OpenStax Chapter Seven on Factoring. Two — Khan Academy's factoring polynomials practice set. Three — do the twelve-problem assignment in your dashboard, mixing all four methods. Four — book time with your advisor if any technique still feels fuzzy. Next up — Module Fourteen, Quadratic Equations, where factoring becomes a SOLVING tool. You're about to see why we put in this work.
